persons.arguments_against: Smith is the commonest surname in the corpus (612 staff-list officials), so the strongest case against is a namesake P. J. C. Smith in Bermuda, or an OCR coincidence on the initials. This fails: the office is not generic — Solicitor-General of Bermuda is a single-occupant senior law office — and the biography records exactly that appointment ('solr.-gen., Berm., 1952') one year before the stint begins. All three initials P. J. C. match, the editions co-occur exactly (both 1953 and 1954, the same two editions the biography appears in), and the career shape fits: called to the bar at Gray's Inn, aged 31 at appointment. A successor-in-post hypothesis fails because the stint starts with the appointment, not after it. No competing biography is in the case.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of two contemporaneous P. J. C. Smiths in the Bermuda establishment, or a record showing the Solicitor-General appointed in 1952 had different forenames.

notes: Three-initial exact match, single-occupant office (Solicitor-General, Bermuda) matching the bio's 1952 appointment, perfect edition co-occurrence (1953, 1954). No live counter-hypothesis.
